summary: Gruber systematically deconstructs rumors about a 10.5-inch iPad launching in early 2017, arguing the timing makes no product-marketing sense. He points out that releasing an edge-to-edge iPad alongside updated versions of existing models would instantly make the updates look dated, cannibalizing their appeal. Applying Occam's razor, he concludes the 10.5-inch iPad is more likely a 2018 product that will inherit the edge-to-edge design language from the rumored new iPhone. He extends the same logic to iPhone rumors, questioning how Apple could sell premium 'S' updates alongside a radical new edge-to-edge flagship without creating product-line incoherence. The analysis draws on supply chain reports from Barclays, Ming-Chi Kuo, and Mac Otakara, finding internal contradictions in their timelines. Gruber's core method is evaluating rumors not on sourcing but on whether they survive contact with Apple's product marketing logic.
key_points: A 10.5-inch edge-to-edge iPad launching alongside updated 9.7-inch and 12.9-inch models creates an impossible product marketing conflict — no one would buy the old-design updates
Apple's historical pattern is to keep new models at existing prices and slot old hardware into lower tiers, not to simultaneously ship new and updated versions of competing form factors
The most logical explanation is that the 10.5-inch iPad is a 2018 product inheriting the edge-to-edge design from the new iPhone, not a 2017 product that debuts the design before the iPhone
The same product-line coherence problem applies to iPhone: a radical new edge-to-edge iPhone only makes sense alongside minor updates to existing sizes, not major 'S' upgrades at premium prices
Ming-Chi Kuo's own report mentions a 'revolutionary' iPad with AMOLED in 2018, which contradicts launching the edge-to-edge redesign in 2017
money_quotes: Who would buy the updated 9.7-inch iPad Pro with the traditional bezels if there's a 10.5-inch model without bezels? No one.
The ideal form factor for iPads and iPhones is just a screen — reducing the size of bezels and moving toward edge-to-edge displays is inevitable.
The new 12.9-inch iPad Pro would look dated on day one.
It doesn't make much sense to me that Apple would introduce a new 10.5-inch iPad Pro in 2017 and 'radical changes in form factor design' in 2018.
Among all the other aforementioned things that don't make sense regarding the rumor that a 10.5-inch iPad is imminent is the idea that the new design language would debut on an iPad, not an iPhone.
If Apple announces all these new iPads at the same time, you have the same problems mentioned above.
key_insight: Rumors must survive contact with product marketing logic, and a 10.5-inch edge-to-edge iPad launching alongside updated old-design models fails that test — making it far more likely a 2018 product that follows the iPhone's design lead.
